revision.text:
{{Expand Japanese|宇美八幡宮|date=September 2011}} {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Umi Hachimangū
宇美八幡宮 | image = | caption = | width = | type = | dedication = [[Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]] | founded = Unknown | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = 1-1-1, Umi, [[Umi, Fukuoka|Umi]]
[[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]] 811-2101 | phone = | website = http://www3.ocn.ne.jp/~umi8man/u_frame_1.htm }} {{Nihongo|'''Umi Hachimangū'''|宇美八幡宮|}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Umi, Fukuoka|Umi]], [[Fukuoka prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is dedicated to [[Emperor Ōjin]] and [[Empress Jingū]]. ==History== The shrine is venerated as the legendary place where [[Empress Jingū]] safely gave birth to the future [[Emperor Ōjin]], and is still patroned by those praying for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing. ==The cluster of giant camphor trees== The cluster of giant [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor trees]] called ''Kada’s Forest'' {{cite web |url=http://www.crossroadfukuoka.jp/en/event/?mode=detail&isSpot=1&id=4000000000373 |title=Umi Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=19 September 2011}} (including the two old camphors that have been deemed [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Treasures]], "Yufuta’s Forest" and "Kinukake’s Forest"). ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== *[http://www3.ocn.ne.jp/~umi8man/u_frame_1.htm Official website] {{ja icon}} *[http://www.crossroadfukuoka.jp/en/event/?mode=detail&isSpot=1&id=4000000000373 Crossroad Fukuoka - Fukuoka Prefecture Tourist Information] {{en icon}} {{coord|33|34|13|N|130|30|32|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark_source:kolossus-dewiki|display=title}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[ja:宇美八幡宮]]

It is dedicated to [[Emperor Ōjin]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]], [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Izanagi]]. In the former [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], it was classified as a prefectural shrine (県社, ''kensha''). ==History== The shrine is venerated as the legendary place where [[Empress Jingū]] safely gave birth to the future [[Emperor Ōjin]], and is still patronized by those praying for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing. ==Natural Treasures== The cluster of giant [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor trees]] called ''Kada’s Forest'' {{cite web |url=http://www.crossroadfukuoka.jp/en/event/?mode=detail&isSpot=1&id=4000000000373 |title=Umi Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=19 September 2011}} (including the two old camphors that have been deemed [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Treasures]], "Yufuta’s Forest" and "Kinukake’s Forest"). ==Gallery== File:Koyasu Stones in Yunokata Shrine and fence of Umi Hachiman Shrine.JPG|''Koyasu-no-ishi'' (子安の石 lit:The praying stone for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing). File:Camphor tree of natural monument Umi-Hachimangu shrine Fukuoka,JAPAN.jpg|''Yufuta's forest'', one of the old camphor trees in this shrine, is designated as the [[Monuments of Japan|National Treasures]]. ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Umi Hachimangū}} *[http://www.umi-hachimangu.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.crossroadfukuoka.jp/en/event/?mode=detail&isSpot=1&id=4000000000373 Crossroad Fukuoka - Fukuoka Prefecture Tourist Information] {{in lang|en}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Hachiman Faith}} {{Authority control}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Umi Hachimangu}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 574]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[en:Umi Hachiman-gū]][[Category:Shrines dedicated to empress Jingū]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia31]] [[en:Umi Hachiman-gū]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia31]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia24]] {{Shinto-stub}}

It is dedicated to [[Emperor Ōjin]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]], [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Izanagi]]. In the former [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], it was classified as a prefectural shrine (県社, ''kensha''). ==History== The shrine is venerated as the legendary place where [[Empress Jingū]] safely gave birth to the future [[Emperor Ōjin]], and is still patronized by those praying for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing. ==Natural Treasures== The cluster of giant [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor trees]] called ''Kada’s Forest'' {{cite web |url=http://www.crossroadfukuoka.jp/en/event/?mode=detail&isSpot=1&id=4000000000373 |title=Umi Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=19 September 2011}} (including the two old camphors that have been deemed [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Treasures]], "Yufuta’s Forest" and "Kinukake’s Forest"). ==Gallery== File:Koyasu Stones in Yunokata Shrine and fence of Umi Hachiman Shrine.JPG|''Koyasu-no-ishi'' (子安の石 lit:The praying stone for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing). [[Izanagi]] | founded_by = | established = Unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-1, Umi, [[Umi, Fukuoka|Umi]]

[[Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]] 811-2101 | website = {{URL|http://www.umi-hachimangu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Umi Hachimangū'''|宇美八幡宮|}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Umi, Fukuoka|Umi]], [[Fukuoka prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is dedicated to [[Emperor Ōjin]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]], [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Izanagi]]. In the former [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]], it was classified as a prefectural shrine (県社, ''kensha''). ==History== The shrine is venerated as the legendary place where [[Empress Jingū]] safely gave birth to the future [[Emperor Ōjin]], and is still patronized by those praying for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing. ==Natural Treasures== The cluster of giant [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor trees]] called ''Kada’s Forest'' {{cite web |url=http://www.crossroadfukuoka.jp/en/event/?mode=detail&isSpot=1&id=4000000000373 |title=Umi Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=19 September 2011}} (including the two old camphors that have been deemed [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Treasures]], "Yufuta’s Forest" and "Kinukake’s Forest"). ==Gallery== File:Koyasu Stones in Yunokata Shrine and fence of Umi Hachiman Shrine.JPG|''Koyasu-no-ishi'' (子安の石 lit:The praying stone for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing). File:Camphor tree of natural monument Umi-Hachimangu shrine Fukuoka,JAPAN.jpg|''Yufuta's forest'', one of the old camphor trees in this shrine, is designated as the [[Monuments of Japan|National Treasures]]. ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Umi Hachimangū}} *[http://www.umi-hachimangu.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.crossroadfukuoka.jp/en/event/?mode=detail&isSpot=1&id=4000000000373 Crossroad Fukuoka - Fukuoka Prefecture Tourist Information] {{in lang|en}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Hachiman Faith}} ]] ]] ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 574]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to empress Jingū]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Umi Hachiman-gū]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Umi Hachiman-gū]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Umi Hachiman-gū]][[Category:qq]] }} [[en:Umi Hachiman-gū]] [[sn:Umi Hachiman-gū]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Umi Hachimangu}}

In the former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], it was classified as a prefectural shrine (県社, ''kensha''). ==History== The shrine is venerated as the legendary place where [[Empress Jingū]] safely gave birth to the future [[Emperor Ōjin]], and is still patronized by those praying for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing. ==Natural Treasures== The cluster of giant [[Cinnamomum camphora|camphor trees]] called ''Kada’s Forest'' {{cite web |url=http://www.crossroadfukuoka.jp/en/event/?mode=detail&isSpot=1&id=4000000000373 |title=Umi Shrine |publisher=[[Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=19 September 2011}} (including the two old camphors that have been deemed [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Treasures]], "Yufuta’s Forest" and "Kinukake’s Forest"). ==Gallery== File:Koyasu Stones in Yunokata Shrine and fence of Umi Hachiman Shrine.JPG|''Koyasu-no-ishi'' (子安の石 lit:The praying stone for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing). File:Camphor tree of natural monument Umi-Hachimangu shrine Fukuoka,JAPAN.jpg|''Yufuta's forest'', one of the old camphor trees in this shrine, is designated as the [[Monuments of Japan|National Treasures]]. ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Umi Hachimangū}} *[http://www.umi-hachimangu.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.crossroadfukuoka.jp/en/event/?mode=detail&isSpot=1&id=4000000000373 Crossroad Fukuoka - Fukuoka Prefecture Tourist Information] {{in lang|en}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Hachiman Faith}} ]] ]] ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 574]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to empress Jingū]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Umi Hachiman-gū]] [[en:Umi Hachiman-gū]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Umi Hachimangū}} [[fr:Umi Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:宇美八幡宮]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าอูมิฮาจิมัง]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

宇美八幡宮 | image = Umi-Hachimangu shrine Fukuoka,JAPAN.jpg | caption = [[Torii]] at Umi Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|34|13|N|130|30|32|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark_source:kolossus-dewiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]]
[[Empress Jingū]]
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]]
[[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]
[[Izanagi]] | founded_by = | established = Unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-1, Umi, [[Umi, Fukuoka|Umi]]
[[Kasuya District, Fukuoka|Kasuya District]]
[[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka]] 811-2101 | website = {{URL|http://www.umi-hachimangu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Umi Hachimangū'''|宇美八幡宮|}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in [[Umi, Fukuoka|Umi]], [[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]], [[Japan]]. It is dedicated to [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], [[Empress Jingū]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]], [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Izanagi]]. In the former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], it was classified as a prefectural shrine (県社, ''kensha''). ==History== The shrine is venerated as the legendary place where [[Empress Jingū]] safely gave birth to the future [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], and is still patronized by those praying for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing. ==Natural Treasures== The cluster of giant [[:en:Cinnamomum camphora|camphor trees]] called ''Kada’s Forest'' {{cite web |url=http://www.crossroadfukuoka.jp/en/event/?mode=detail&isSpot=1&id=4000000000373 |title=Umi Shrine |publisher=[[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=19 September 2011}} (including the two old camphors that have been deemed [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Treasures]], "Yufuta’s Forest" and "Kinukake’s Forest"). ==Gallery== File:Koyasu Stones in Yunokata Shrine and fence of Umi Hachiman Shrine.JPG|''Koyasu-no-ishi'' (子安の石 lit:The praying stone for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing). File:Camphor tree of natural monument Umi-Hachimangu shrine Fukuoka,JAPAN.jpg|''Yufuta's forest'', one of the old camphor trees in this shrine, is designated as the [[Monuments of Japan|National Treasures]]. ==See also== *[[List of Shinto shrines]] ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Umi Hachimangū}} *[http://www.umi-hachimangu.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.crossroadfukuoka.jp/en/event/?mode=detail&isSpot=1&id=4000000000373 Crossroad Fukuoka - Fukuoka Prefecture Tourist Information] {{in lang|en}} {{Shinto shrine}} {{Hachiman Faith}} ]] ]] ]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 574]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shrines dedicated to empress Jingū]] }} [[sn:Umi Hachiman-gū]] [[en:Umi Hachiman-gū]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] {{Commons category|Umi Hachimangū}} [[fr:Umi Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:宇美八幡宮]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าอูมิฮาจิมัง]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]]

It is dedicated to [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]], [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]], [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Izanagi]]. In the former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], it was classified as a prefectural shrine (県社, ''kensha''). ==History== The shrine is venerated as the legendary place where [[:en:Empress Jingū|Empress Jingū]] safely gave birth to the future [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], and is still patronized by those praying for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing. ==Natural Treasures== The cluster of giant [[:en:Cinnamomum camphora|camphor trees]] called ''Kada’s Forest'' {{cite web |url=http://www.crossroadfukuoka.jp/en/event/?mode=detail&isSpot=1&id=4000000000373 |title=Umi Shrine |publisher=[[:en:Fukuoka Prefecture|Fukuoka Prefecture]] |accessdate=19 September 2011}} (including the two old camphors that have been deemed [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Treasures]], "Yufuta’s Forest" and "Kinukake’s Forest"). ==Gallery== File:Koyasu Stones in Yunokata Shrine and fence of Umi Hachiman Shrine.JPG|''Koyasu-no-ishi'' (子安の石 lit:The praying stone for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing). [[co:commons:Category:Umi Hachimangū]][[en:Umi Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Umi Hachiman-gū]][[ja:宇美八幡宮]][[th:ศาลเจ้าอูมิฮาจิมัง]] [[co:commons:Category:Umi Hachimangū]][[en:Umi Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Umi Hachiman-gū]][[ja:宇美八幡宮]][[th:ศาลเจ้าอูมิฮาจิมัง]] [[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Umi Hachimangū]][[en:Umi Hachiman-gū]][[fr:Umi Hachiman-gū]][[ja:宇美八幡宮]][[th:ศาลเจ้าอูมิฮาจิมัง]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Fukuoka Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Umi Hachimangū
宇美八幡宮 | image = Umi-Hachimangu shrine Fukuoka,JAPAN.jpg | caption = [[Torii]] at Umi Hachimangū | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|33|34|13|N|130|30|32|E|region:JP-40_type:landmark_source:kolossus-dewiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}
{{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}
[[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]]
[[Sumiyoshi sanjin]]
[[Izanagi]] | founded_by = | established = Unknown | date_destroyed = | location = 1-1-1, Umi, {{ill|Umi, Fukuoka|en|Umi, Fukuoka|WD=Q945518|lt=Umi|1=Umi, Fukuoka}}
{{ill|Kasuya District, Fukuoka|en|Kasuya District, Fukuoka|WD=Q1206294|lt=Kasuya District|1=Kasuya District, Fukuoka}}
{{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}} 811-2101 | website = {{URL|http://www.umi-hachimangu.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Umi Hachimangū'''|宇美八幡宮|}} is a [[Shinto]] {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Umi, Fukuoka|en|Umi, Fukuoka|WD=Q945518|lt=Umi|1=Umi, Fukuoka}}, {{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It is dedicated to {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}}, [[Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)|Tamayori-bime]], [[Sumiyoshi sanjin]] and [[Izanagi]]. In the former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]], it was classified as a prefectural shrine (県社, ''kensha''). ==History== The shrine is venerated as the legendary place where {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} safely gave birth to the future {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, and is still patronized by those praying for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing. ==Natural Treasures== The cluster of giant [[:en:Cinnamomum camphora|camphor trees]] called ''Kada’s Forest'' {{cite web |url=http://www.crossroadfukuoka.jp/en/event/?mode=detail&isSpot=1&id=4000000000373 |title=Umi Shrine |publisher={{ill|Fukuoka Prefecture|en|Fukuoka Prefecture|WD=Q123258|lt=Fukuoka Prefecture|1=Fukuoka Prefecture}} |accessdate=19 September 2011}} (including the two old camphors that have been deemed [[Monuments of Japan|Natural Treasures]], "Yufuta’s Forest" and "Kinukake’s Forest"). ==Gallery== File:Koyasu Stones in Yunokata Shrine and fence of Umi Hachiman Shrine.JPG|''Koyasu-no-ishi'' (子安の石 lit:The praying stone for safe childbirth and smooth child rearing). File:Camphor tree of natural monument Umi-Hachimangu shrine Fukuoka,JAPAN.jpg|''Yufuta's forest'', one of the old camphor trees in this shrine, is designated as the [[Monuments of Japan|National Treasures]]. ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons Category|Umi Hachimangū}} *[http://www.umi-hachimangu.or.jp/ Official website] {{in lang|ja}} *[http://www.crossroadfukuoka.jp/en/event/?mode=detail&isSpot=1&id=4000000000373 Crossroad Fukuoka - Fukuoka Prefecture Tourist Information] {{in lang|en}} {{Hachiman Faith}} ]]{{Shinto-stub}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Umi Hachiman-gū]] {{Commons category|Umi Hachimangū}} [[fr:Umi Hachiman-gū]] [[ja:宇美八幡宮]] [[th:ศาลเจ้าอูมิฮาจิมัง]] {{wikidata link|Q924940}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Hachiman shrines]] [[Category:Ken-sha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Fukuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
